---
title: "Plan & Design"
description: "Explore existing resources, write data management plans, design studies, and prepare preregistrations"
phase_order: 1
sidebar: open-research-cycle
page-layout: full
css: orc-styles.css
title-block-style: none
---

<div class="orc-phase-hero-compact">
<div class="orc-hero-cycle phase-1">
{{< include ../images/os-cycle.svg >}}
</div>
<div class="orc-hero-body">
<div class="orc-hero-tagline">Set the foundation for open & reliable research</div>
<div class="orc-hero-pills">
<span><i class="bi bi-check2-circle"></i> Explore & Reuse</span> <!-- make the boxes around not so tall so they don't touch if two lines --> 
<span><i class="bi bi-check2-circle"></i> Check Legal Frameworks</span>
<span><i class="bi bi-check2-circle"></i> Write Data Management Plan</span>
<span><i class="bi bi-check2-circle"></i> Design Study</span>
</div>
<div class="orc-hero-checkpoint"><i class="bi bi-people-fill"></i> Team Checkpoint: Study Plan Presentation & Preregistration</div>
</div>
</div>


<details class="orc-details-panel">
<summary><i class="bi bi-search"></i> Explore and Reuse</summary> <!-- make this. and the other top titles, a sharable url -->

Any resource that inspire you or that you want to reuse and/or adapt must minimally be cited, using their DOIs (and otherwise URL with author, date, and time of access) and you must follow the license and/or usage agreement provided by the authors. A research output (e.g. data, code) without a license cannot be reused even if they appear publicly online.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Articles <!--make this, and other tabs title everywhere, a shareable url? -->

* To come up with a well-founded research question, **review existing literature** on open source registries like [OpenAlex](https://openalex.org/). 
* Keep track of your bibliography with an open source **reference manager** like [Zotero](https://www.zotero.org/). It can be integrated within RStudio to write reproducible manuscripts seamlessly, formatting your bibliography in any format you want (see [3. Analyze & Collaborate](03_analyze_and_collaborate.qmd)). <!-- url of link will change when merging to main website -does this currently work?? -->  <!-- could this be not looking like a hyperlink for an external page but maybe a small grey italic button looking text or something less popping out --> 


::: {.orc-two-column} 
<!-- I want these cards to be clickable anywhere to link to the tutorial/tool - not just the title, and be of the same size --> 

::: {.orc-column}
#### <i class="bi bi-mortarboard"></i> LEARN MORE

<div class="orc-card-tool"><!-- should the logo here be of zotero, and the OSC logo integrated in the corner tag? or two logos, the OSC logo and the tool logo on the left? --> 
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon"><img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" alt="LMU OSC logo"></div>
<h4>[Introduction to Zotero](https://lmu-osc.github.io/introduction-to-zotero/)</h4>
<p>An open-source reference manager that integrates with Word, RStudio, and Google Docs. (1h)</p>
<a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/introduction-to-zotero/" class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial</a>
</div>
:::

::: {.orc-column}
#### <i class="bi bi-tools"></i> TOOLS & RESOURCES <!-- this shouldn't be a link / anchor / url making title -->

<div class="orc-card-tool">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon"><img src="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/OpenAlex_2025_favicon.svg/250px-OpenAlex_2025_favicon.svg.png" alt="OpenAlex icon"></div>
<h4>[OpenAlex](https://openalex.org/)</h4>
<p>All the world's research, connected and open</p>
</div>
:::

:::



### Preregistrations

A **preregistration** typically consists of a hypothesis and predictions, a plan for data collection (when relevant), and a plan for data analysis, that researchers upload before starting their projects, often in order to increase the rigor of confirmatory research (see [Study Design & Analysis plan](###study-design--analysis-plan)) <!-- this cross ref is not working, it's not a md title FIXME -->

<!-- I want a line break here for fuck sake -->
<br>

To have some **insight into projects that are not (yet) published**, either currently ongoing or abandoned, you can therefore look for projects that were preregistered. Projects that are left unpublished typically have a note attached to their preregistration. Some registries are discipline-specific while others are discipline-agnostic:

<div style="margin-top: 1em;"> <!--or make them all clickable cards? -->
* <https://osf.io/registries> <!-- this randomly appears as a list OR NOT - can't explain -->
* <https://aspredicted.org/>
* <https://preclinicaltrials.eu/>
* <https://www.animalstudyregistry.org/>
* <https://clinicaltrials.gov/>
</div>


#### <i class="bi bi-tools"></i> TOOLS & RESOURCES

::: {.orc-grid-tools}

<div class="orc-card-tool">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon"><img src="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Open_Science_Framework_log_%28and_Center_for_Open_Science_logo%29.svg/3840px-Open_Science_Framework_log_%28and_Center_for_Open_Science_logo%29.svg.png" alt="OSF"></div>
<h4>[OSF](https://osf.io/registries/)</h4>
<p>Registry of preregistrations. Widely used across fields.</p>
</div>
:::

### Data

To find **existing datasets**, search for discipline-specific repositories on [re3data](https://www.re3data.org/) which is a central registry of all repositories, or explore subject agnostic repositories such as [DataCite](https://datacite.org/) or [Zenodo](https://zenodo.org/) (oftentimes with the corresponding analysis code). These platforms either give you access to existing data or provide metadata and explanations on how to request access to the data.

<!-- add zenodo card as tool-->
::: {.orc-grid-tools}

<div class="orc-card-tool">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon"><i class="bi bi-database"></i></div>
<h4>[re3data](https://www.re3data.org/)</h4>
<p>Registry of data repositories. Search across disciplines to find domain-specific options.</p>
</div>

:::


### Code

 You may also find **code available for reuse** on [Zenodo](https://zenodo.org/) (archived) or actively developed on [Github](https://github.com/). Learn how to work with GitHub in more details in [3. Analyze & Collaborate](03_analyze_and_collaborate.qmd) or start learning Git version control now! 
 
 <br>

 Important: Code publicly visible on GitHub without a license cannot be legally reused. Ask the authors to add an **open license** to their repository to allow reuse (they must add a file called LICENSE.txt with e.g. the Apache 2.0 license text - see our [code publishing tutorial](https://lmu-osc.github.io/code-publishing/choose_license.html) to learn more about licenses)

<!--  add github tutorials in learn more, add github as a tool - remove gitlab for now-->
<!-- add zenodo card in tool-->
::: {.orc-grid-tools}
<div class="orc-card-tool">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon"><img src="https://cdn.simpleicons.org/github" alt="GitHub"></div>
<h4>[GitHub](https://github.com/) / [GitLab](https://gitlab.com/)</h4>
<p>Version control platforms for code collaboration with issue tracking.</p> <!-- change sentence-->
<a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/Introduction-RStudio-Git-GitHub/" class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">Tutorial</a>
</div>
:::

:::

</details>


<details class="orc-details-panel">
<summary><i class="bi bi-shield-check"></i> Legal Requirements</summary>

::: {.panel-tabset}

### LMU guidelines

The LMU [Guidelines for Safeguarding Good Scientific Practice](https://cms-cdn.lmu.de/media/contenthub/amtliche-veroeffentlichungen/gwp-ordnung.pdf) (in german) are legally binding for all academics, researchers, research support staff, teachers, and students at LMU Munich. Only the original text in German prevails, but here is an English summary of relevant aspects of this guide:

<em>
<h4>Appropriate level of documentation and standards to allow **reproduction**:</h4>

* Reproducible methods must be used. (§11)
* When research software is developed, its source code must be documented. (§12)

<h4>Appropriate level of documentation and standards to allow **replication**:</h4>

* All information relevant to the production of a research result must be documented comprehensively to enable replication. (§7 and §12)
* If specific professional recommendations exist for review and evaluation, the results must be documented in accordance with these respective specifications. (§12) 
* Individual results that do not support the hypothesis must also be documented; a selection of results is not permitted. (§12)

<h4>Public access to research results:</h4>

* Apart from specific exceptions, all findings should be made public. For this, they must be described in a detailed and comprehensible manner which includes making available the research data, materials and information on which the results are based, as well as the methods used and the software employed (including appropriately licensed self-written software) according to the FAIR principles*. (§13)
* Data, material, software made publicly accessible must be appropriately archived, usually for a period of 10 years (§17).

</em>

*Note: The FAIR principles are defined as: <!-- maintain the freaking list formatting... --> <!-- maybe in a info box -->
- Findable: metadata should be deposited in a searchable repository and be assigned a permanent identifier
- Accessible: the data is either open, or accessible upon some authentication process, or closed, but with open metadata.
- Interoperable: the data is described with a standard terminology (so the dataset can be merged with other ones) and saved in a stable file format
- Reusable: the data is richly documented (e.g. with a data dictionary) and is accompanied by a data usage license
See https://www.go-fair.org/fair-principles/ for more information and section [2. Collect & Manage](02_collect_and_manage.qmd) to learn how to implement the FAIR principles in your research.


<!-- add a card with LMU logo and LMU Good Scientific Practices as title with link to pdf? in section Resources-->
<!-- add FAIR data management tutorial in learn more section ?-->


### Funders

Funders may have additional open and reproducible science requirement on top of those indicated in the LMU guidelines. For instance, some calls request a Research Data Management plan before making their second payment, some specify the extent and timing of data sharing and provide funds for such activity. Check all requirements in the call information sheets.

<br>

Contact the [LMU Research Funding Unit](https://www.lmu.de/en/research/research-services/contact.html) to review your grant proposal and assess if you have met your funders' open science requirements. 

### Ethics

Data collection and analyses conducted on animals and humans typically requires the approval of your Faculty's ethics committee (see e.g. [instructions for the LMU Faculty of Medicine](https://www.med.lmu.de/de/fakultaet/wer-wir-sind/gremien-kommissionen/ethikkommission/) and [instructions for the LMU Department of Psychology](https://www.lmu.de/psyedu/en/research/ethics-committee/#:~:text=The%20Ethics%20Committee%20advises%20and,and%20in%20security%2Drelevant%20areas.)) to make sure the research will be conducted responsibly and participants’ data will be protected.

<br>

#### <i class="bi bi-list-check"></i> Your ethics proposal will typically include: <!-- remove ulr forming link -->

* **Data storage and retention** – outlining how data will be securely stored, backed up, and retained over time. This is usually part of a detailed Research Data Management plan (see next section for guidance<!--cross ref-->).
* **Risks if the data were leaked** – identifying potential consequences for participants or the research project if confidentiality is breached.
* **Data anonymisation** – describing procedures to remove or obscure personally identifiable information to protect participant privacy.
* **Language of the informed consent forms** – ensuring that participants clearly understand the purpose, procedures, and any potential risks of the study. Consent for sharing their data and under which conditions should be clearly explained here.
* **Power analysis** to justify sample size – providing a statistical rationale for the number of participants, which supports the validity and ethical justification of the study.

<!-- add cards for RDM plan chapter, data anonymisation tutorial, power analyses tutorial-->
<!-- add example consent form text from Felix??-->



:::

</details>



<details class="orc-details-panel">
<summary><i class="bi bi-lightbulb"></i> <i class="bi bi-clipboard-check"></i> Research Data Management Plans</summary><!--change logo - see folder with tick in the website version -->

A Data Management Plan (DMP) documents how you will handle research data throughout your project. Write it before your ethics submission, since the content overlaps significantly and most funders require one. Writing a DMP prompts you to think through decisions you might otherwise leave implicit, and documents them using standard terminology.

#### <i class="bi bi-list-check"></i> Your DMP will address:

- **What data** will you collect or generate (types, formats, volume, sources)?
- **How will you describe it** (metadata standards, documentation practices)?
- **How will you organize files** (naming conventions, folder structure, versioning)?
- **Where will you store it** (locations, backups, access controls)?
- **How will you ensure quality** (validation checks, error-handling)?
- **How will you share outputs** (repositories, licenses, embargo periods)?
- **What constraints apply** (consent, anonymization, GDPR, data use agreements)?

The specific questions vary by discipline, data type, and funder requirements. DMP tools like RDMO guide you through the relevant questions with funder-specific templates. Your DMP is a living document: start with what you know, and the rest of the cycle will help you refine the details as your project develops.

<!-- cut this sentence but add RIO in section tools and resources for examples DMP to peruse by discipline-->
**See example DMPs:** [DMPonline public plans](https://dmponline.dcc.ac.uk/public_plans) | [Argos published DMPs](https://argos.openaire.eu/explore-plans) | [RIOjournal DMPs](https://riojournal.com/browse_journal_articles.php?form_name=filter_articles&selfurl=&backurl=&sortby=0&journal_id=17&search_hidden=&search_in_=0&search_in_hidden=&alerts_subject_cats=&alerts_sd498444d0a_publishing_status=&section_type%5B%5D=231)

::: {.orc-two-column}

::: {.orc-column}
#### <i class="bi bi-mortarboard"></i> LEARN MORE

<div class="orc-card-tool">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon"><img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" alt="LMU OSC"></div>
<h4>[FAIR Data Management](https://lmu-osc.github.io/FAIR-Data-Management/)</h4>
<p>Includes a chapter on writing DMPs (2h total)</p>
<a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/FAIR-Data-Management/dmp.html" class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">DMP chapter</a>
</div>
:::

::: {.orc-column}
#### <i class="bi bi-tools"></i> TOOLS

<div class="orc-card-tool">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon"><img src="https://rdmo.readthedocs.io/en/latest/_static/RDMO-logo.png" alt="RDMO"></div>
<h4>[RDMO](https://rdmo.ub.lmu.de/)</h4>
<p>Funder-compliant templates (DFG, ERC, Horizon Europe)</p>
<a href="https://rdmo.ub.lmu.de/" class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">LMU supported</a>
</div>
:::

:::

</details>



<details class="orc-details-panel">
<summary><i class="bi bi-graph-up"></i> Study Design & Analysis Plan</summary><!-- I want to cross ref this title - how? --> 
<!-- logo like https://ree-gupta.github.io/lmu-osc.github.io/training/open_research_cycle/01_plan_and_design_website.html#planning-framework-options -->

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Pre-analysis planning

3 lines of text

::: {.orc-grid-tools}

<div class="orc-card-tool">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon"><img src="https://cdn.simpleicons.org/osf" alt="OSF"></div>
<h4>[OSF](https://osf.io/)</h4>
<p>Flexible templates, embargoes, file storage. Widely used across fields.</p>
</div>
:::

### Simulation of Data

3 lines of text



### Power analyses

3 lines of text


:::


</details>

## <i class="bi bi-clipboard-check"></i> Planning Checklist 
<!-- format like in 'website version' but with smaller title so it fits with the new 4 titles above -->

::: {.orc-gradient-box}

**Research Design:**

<div class="orc-grid-2col">

<div>

- [ ] Research questions clearly defined
- [ ] Hypotheses specified (if applicable)
- [ ] Study design chosen and justified
- [ ] Variables and measures identified
</div>

<div>

- [ ] Sample size determined with power analysis
- [ ] Inclusion/exclusion criteria defined
- [ ] Potential confounds considered
- [ ] Ethical approval obtained (if required)
</div>

</div>

**Documentation & Planning:**

<div class="orc-grid-2col">

<div>

- [ ] Data Management Plan drafted
- [ ] Preregistration prepared (if applicable)
- [ ] Analysis plan documented
- [ ] File naming conventions established
</div>

<div>

- [ ] Repository/storage solution selected
- [ ] Collaboration tools set up
- [ ] Timeline and milestones defined
- [ ] Budget includes open science costs
</div>

</div>

**Before Data Collection:**

- [ ] All study materials finalized and documented
- [ ] Preregistration submitted (if applicable)
- [ ] Data collection protocols tested
- [ ] Team roles and responsibilities clear

:::
